topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with the usual midday chatter, a backdrop to the simmering frustration tightening in my chest. I watched Liam across the small table, his easy smile directed at someone else, completely oblivious to the shift in the atmosphere I was cultivating. He reached across the table to grab a pastry, and I felt a sharp, unwelcome clench in my jaw. "Hey, Liam," I began, my voice deliberately level, "Remember that camping trip to the lake? The one where we tried to build that ridiculously unstable raft?" He blinked, a momentary flicker of confusion crossing his face before he offered a vague, "Vaguely. A lot of summer." The casual dismissal hit me like a physical blow. I gripped my mug so tightly my knuckles turned white. *How could he not remember that? It was a cornerstone of our entire shared history.* I wanted to shout, to demand a clearer memory, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a tight, hot pressure behind my eyes.

[story 2]

I stood by the lockers, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead mirroring the frantic rhythm inside my own head. Every time I thought of Sarah, of the countless whispered secrets and shared triumphs of our youth, a wave of hot anger washed over me. I watched her laugh with Mark, a sound that used to be the soundtrack to my best days, and a sudden, cold detachment settled over me. "Sarah," I said, my tone sharper than intended, "Do you recall that disastrous school play? The one where the curtain kept falling?" She paused, tilting her head, her expression one of polite blankness. "Oh, I think so? Something about drama?" The way she glossed over the memory, treating it like a minor footnote, made my stomach twist uncomfortably. I pushed off the lockers, the sound of my own footsteps echoing loudly in the hallway. I needed an answer, a recognition that the depth of what we’d shared actually existed for her too.

[story 3]

The silence in the room felt heavy, thick enough to choke on. I leaned forward, resting my elbows on the table, my gaze locked onto Alex. I had rehearsed this conversation a hundred times in my head, trying to find the right cadence to extract the truth. "Alex," I started, my voice pitched low, carrying an edge of disbelief, "The summer we spent trying to learn how to surf? Remember that? We almost drowned twice." Alex shifted uncomfortably in his seat, running a hand through his hair. "I... I don't think so," he admitted, his tone genuinely perplexed. "I feel like I know you, but that specific memory isn't… sticking." A strange heat rose in my cheeks, a fierce, burning sensation that made me want to physically recoil. I stared at him, my expression carefully neutral, but the tension radiating off me was palpable. *He can’t even place that? After everything?* I wanted to shake him, to force him to see the undeniable reality of our past together.
